name: Los 10,000 del Soplao Road Route
name_original: Cabezón de la Sal, San Vicente de la Barquera, Collado de Ozalba, Collado de Carmona – ruta circular
description: This difficult 67.4-mile (108.4 km) road cycling route in Cantabria offers challenging climbs and diverse scenery, including coastal views.
region_intro: Road cycling around Cabezón De La Sal offers a diverse landscape characterized by open valleys, mountainous terrain, and proximity to the Cantabrian coast. The region features the Saja River, which carves through the Valle de Cabezón, and is bordered by the Sierra del Escudo de Cabuérniga to the south. This varied topography provides a range of road cycling routes, from valley sections to significant mountain passes. The area also includes unique natural features like the Monumento Natural de las Secuoyas. Best road cycling routes around Cabezón De La Sal The most popular road cycling route is Los 10,000 del Soplao Road Route, a 67.4 miles (108.4 km) difficult trail that takes 5 hours 59 minutes to complete. This route offers diverse scenery and coastal views, including significant elevation gain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Palombera Pass – Collada de Carmona loop from Cabezón de la Sal, a difficult 55.1 miles (88.6 km) path. This route navigates through mountainous terrain, featuring notable passes and loops. Local road cyclists also love the Section between sea and mountains – Views of the Zapedo marsh loop from Cabezón de la Sal, a 25.9 miles (41.7 km) trail leading through coastal and mountainous vistas, often completed in about 2 hours 7 minutes. Road cycling around Cabezón De La Sal is defined by open valleys, challenging mountain passes, and coastal sections with views of marshes. The network offers options for different ability levels, including easy, moderate, and difficult routes. The routes in Cabezón De La Sal are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.9 stars from over 7 reviews. More than 800 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Cabezón De La Sal's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling around Cabezón de la Sal?
A: The region offers a diverse landscape for road cycling, characterized by open valleys, mountainous terrain, and proximity to the Cantabrian coast. You'll find routes that follow the Saja River through the Valle de Cabezón, as well as challenging climbs into the Sierra del Escudo de Cabuérniga. This variety ensures options from relatively flat sections to significant mountain passes.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling climbs in the Cabezón de la Sal area?
A: Yes, Cabezón de la Sal is known for its demanding climbs and is even a key location for events like 'La Ruta de los 10.000 del Soplao'. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1005116747/los-10-000-del-soplao-road-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los 10,000 del Soplao Road Route&lt;/a&gt; feature significant elevation gains, and the area includes mountain passes such as Lunada, considered one of Spain's most beautiful and strenuous.

Q: Are there any easier road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Cabezón de la Sal?
A: While many routes are challenging, the area does offer options for different ability levels. For a moderate ride that combines coastal and mountainous vistas, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40330011" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Section between sea and mountains – Views of the Zapedo marsh loop from Cabezón de la Sal&lt;/a&gt;, which is about 41.7 km long.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while road cycling in Cabezón de la Sal?
A: You can encounter a variety of natural attractions. The region is home to the unique &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5862315" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Oyambre Natural Park&lt;/a&gt;, offering stunning coastal views, and the Monumento Natural de las Secuoyas, a protected area with majestic Coast Redwoods. The Saja River also carves through the Valle de Cabezón, providing scenic riverside sections.

Q: Are there scenic viewpoints along the road cycling routes in Cabezón de la Sal?
A: Absolutely. Many routes offer breathtaking views, especially those that venture into the mountainous areas or along the coast. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40330011" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Section between sea and mountains – Views of the Zapedo marsh loop&lt;/a&gt; provides coastal and marshland vistas, while routes through passes like Palombera and Carmona offer expansive mountain panoramas.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Cabezón de la Sal?
A: The diverse landscape of Cabezón de la Sal makes it suitable for cycling across various seasons. However, for optimal conditions, spring and autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures and less extreme weather, ideal for tackling both valley routes and mountain passes. Summer can also be excellent, especially for coastal rides, though mountain climbs might be warmer.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes available from Cabezón de la Sal?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes in the area are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40345988" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Palombera Pass – Collada de Carmona loop from Cabezón de la Sal&lt;/a&gt; and the scenic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40330011" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Section between sea and mountains – Views of the Zapedo marsh loop&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Cabezón de la Sal?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.9 stars from over 7 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse scenery, which ranges from open valleys and challenging mountain passes to stunning coastal views, and the extensive network of routes catering to various fitness levels.

Q: Are there any historical sites or towns I can visit near the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, Cabezón de la Sal's location allows for easy access to several notable historical sites. You could visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/360503" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santillana del Mar Historic Town Centre&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/344751" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Historic center of Comillas&lt;/a&gt;, both renowned for their architectural beauty and cultural heritage.

Q: How many road cycling routes are there around Cabezón de la Sal?
A: Komoot features over 20 road cycling routes around Cabezón de la Sal, offering a wide range of distances and difficulties. This extensive network ensures there's a suitable path for almost any cyclist, from moderate loops to very difficult, long-distance challenges.

Q: Are there any long-distance road cycling routes originating from Cabezón de la Sal?
A: Yes, the region is a starting point for several longer and more challenging routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1005116747/los-10-000-del-soplao-road-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los 10,000 del Soplao Road Route&lt;/a&gt; covers over 108 km with significant elevation, and even longer routes like 'Soplao Clásica 224km' originate from the area.

Q: What are some of the notable mountain passes for road cycling in this region?
A: The Cabezón de la Sal area is known for its challenging mountain passes, or 'colladas'. Key passes include Palombera and Carmona, which are featured in routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40345988" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Palombera Pass – Collada de Carmona loop&lt;/a&gt;. The region also boasts Lunada, considered one of Spain's most beautiful climbs.
tips: With 500 meters in length, the bridge connects the town across the length and breadth of the San Vicente estuary. The first bridge was made of wood; then a stone one was built in the 15th century. On this, the current one was finished, during the reign of Carlos III. It represents the most traditional access road to reach San Vicente de la Barquera. When it was built in medieval times, it was listed as one of the most impressive works of naval engineering. There are privileged views of the estuary.

It is a fishing and fishing village surrounded by a privileged environment, in the heart of the Oyambre Natural Park. It has beautiful beaches such as Oyambre and the extensive sandy areas of Merón, small coves such as Gerra, without forgetting the San Vicente estuary, the Tostadero estuary and Fuentes.

The section between Linares and La Hermida will be a great surprise. It is a winding mountain road that will leave you with tired legs and a feeling of joy at being able to enjoy such unparalleled views.

On the way down, I suggest you visit the Romanesque church of Santa Juliana, right at the entrance to the town of Lafuente.
Great paths for biking and the views are spectacular! Almost free of traffic and in perfect condition. Watch out for cows and cow pats on the road!

faqs.question: What is the estimated cycling time for this route?
faqs.answer: The estimated cycling time for this route is around 6 hours. However, this is for the cycling portion only; allow extra time for breaks, enjoying the scenery, and any stops you might make along the way.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as a difficult road ride, requiring very good fitness. It features significant elevation gain and a considerable distance, making it more suitable for experienced cyclists.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this circular route?
faqs.answer: Cabezón de la Sal is a common starting point for this circular route. You should be able to find parking within the town, though specific parking areas may vary. San Vicente de la Barquera is another option if you prefer to start closer to the coast.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This is primarily a hard road ride, but be aware that some segments may be unpaved, adding to the challenge. You'll encounter everything from coastal roads near San Vicente de la Barquera to the ascents of Collado de Ozalba and Collado de Carmona, which are known for good road conditions.

faqs.question: What are some notable points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll pass several interesting spots, including the historic Puente de la Maza and the scenic San Vicente de la Barquera Castle and Estuary View. Further inland, you'll experience the dramatic La Hermida Gorge and climb significant passes like Collada de Carmona and Collado de Ozalba.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is typically during spring or autumn when the weather is milder and more stable. Summer can be pleasant, but be prepared for potentially higher temperatures. Winter cycling is possible in Cantabria, but you should check weather conditions carefully, especially for the mountain passes.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: As this is primarily a road cycling route, dogs are generally allowed if they are safely secured in a bike trailer or carrier. However, if you venture onto any unpaved sections or trails, always check local regulations regarding dogs, especially within natural park areas.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need any special permits or pay entrance fees to cycle this route. It follows public roads and passes through natural areas like Parque Natural de Oyambre, which does not typically require permits for general access.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery and wildlife can I expect to see?
faqs.answer: The route offers diverse and picturesque scenery, transitioning from coastal views near San Vicente de la Barquera to charming towns and the majestic natural landscapes of inland valleys. As you head towards Collado de Ozalba, you'll see pleasantly rolling green hills. Keep an eye out for local wildlife and birds, especially in the more rural sections.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route briefly intersects with several other paths. You'll find sections that overlap with the [CIMA CA16] Collado de Hoz * La Hermida, the Senda del Deva, and the Camino Lebaniego, among others.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this challenging road cycling route?
faqs.answer: Given the route's difficulty and length, you should bring plenty of water and snacks, appropriate cycling gear, a repair kit, a first-aid kit, and navigation tools. Weather can change quickly in the mountains, so layers of clothing are advisable.
